resume:
Tomas Reyes
Operations Coordinator — Fulfillment & Logistics

EXPERIENCE
Operations Coordinator, BrightBox Fulfillment — 2023-Present
- Situation: inbound receiving backlog averaged 2 days at the Reno warehouse. Task: cut dock-to-stock time. Action: re-sequenced the receiving queue by SKU velocity and added a midday cycle-count. Result: dock-to-stock dropped from 48h to 14h over one quarter.
- Situation: pick-error rate sat at 1.4%. Task: reduce mispicks on the B-line. Action: introduced scan-verify at pack-out and a daily error-review huddle. Result: mispicks fell to 0.5% in eight weeks.
- Coordinated daily standups for a 30-person floor team across two shifts.

Shift Lead, QuickServe Distribution — 2021-2023
- Situation: peak-season overtime was 22%. Task: smooth labor demand. Action: built a simple volume-forecast sheet and staggered start times. Result: overtime fell to 12% during peak.
- Trained 12 seasonal hires on safety and pick procedures.

EDUCATION
B.S. Supply Chain Management, University of Nevada, Reno — 2021

SKILLS
WMS (Manhattan), Excel, cycle counting, labor scheduling, Lean basics, forklift certified
jd: Vantage Distribution is hiring an Operations Coordinator for our high-volume e-commerce fulfillment center (350k sq ft, 200+ associates across three shifts). Reporting to the Shift Operations Manager, you will run daily floor execution: labor allocation, dock-to-stock flow, pick/pack/ship throughput, and the daily metrics huddle. We expect a coordinator who can diagnose a throughput problem on the floor, isolate the real bottleneck rather than the obvious symptom, and drive a measurable fix. The ideal candidate has 1-3 years in a WMS-driven warehouse, is comfortable with labor-planning math, and can stay composed and clear under peak-season pressure. We will ask you to walk through specific operational problems you've solved — the bottleneck, your intervention, and the numbers — and we value calm, structured communication.

history.q: Tell me about your current role at BrightBox and the size of the operation you support.
Your resume says you cut dock-to-stock from 48 to 14 hours. What was actually causing the backlog?
How did you confirm SKU-velocity sequencing was the right lever and not just adding headcount?
Let's talk about the mispick project. What was driving the 1.4% error rate?
Why scan-verify at pack-out specifically, rather than at the pick face?
Tell me about the overtime work at QuickServe. How did the forecast sheet actually work?
How accurate was that forecast week to week?
Describe a time the floor blew up on you mid-shift — what happened and what did you do?
How did you keep the team calm while you sorted it out?
Walk me through one specific peak day where you had to re-allocate labor in real time — what was the bottleneck, what did you move, and what was the result?

candidate_last_answer: Okay so — sorry, peak day, there was a lot going on that day. Um, so it was Black Friday weekend, the Saturday I think, or maybe it was the Cyber Monday, honestly they kind of blur together during peak. Anyway, the thing is, we had a ton of volume, like way more than the forecast said, and — oh, I should mention our forecast sheet, which I built, it usually was pretty good but that day it was just off. So the floor was backing up at pack-out, or, well, it looked like pack-out but it might've actually been the dock, I remember running back and forth. And I just kind of — you know how it is, you have to triage. So I pulled some people. I think I moved a couple folks from picking over to pack, and we kind of got through it. It was stressful, my manager was asking for numbers and I didn't have them in the moment. But yeah, we shipped most of it, eventually. Sorry, what was the specific part you wanted — the bottleneck or the labor move?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it looked like pack-out but it might've actually been the dock

expected-yield: a clear identification of the actual bottleneck (pack-out vs. dock), the signal he used to locate it, exactly how many people he moved and from where, and the throughput result — cutting through the rambling to a structured account
